pathophysiology_summary: Progressive calcific narrowing of the aortic valve orifice creates a fixed outflow obstruction. As the valve area shrinks, the left ventricle cannot augment cardiac output during exertion, producing the classic symptomatic triad: exertional angina (increased myocardial oxygen demand against a hypertrophied, pressure-overloaded ventricle with relatively fixed coronary flow reserve), exertional syncope/presyncope (fixed stroke volume cannot rise to meet exertional vasodilation, causing a fall in cerebral perfusion), and exertional dyspnoea (rising left ventricular filling pressures from diastolic dysfunction/early failure). Onset of any one of these symptoms in aortic stenosis marks a major prognostic inflection point — average survival without valve replacement falls sharply (roughly 2 years with heart failure, 3 years with syncope, 5 years with angina) once symptoms develop, with a materially elevated risk of sudden cardiac death. The physical examination tetrad (harsh ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids, slow-rising/small-volume carotid pulse, narrowed pulse pressure, sustained apical impulse) is the clinician-side confirmation of the mechanism, but none of it is obtainable via chat — the telehealth task is to recognise the symptom pattern and escalate, not to confirm the valvular lesion.

differential.evidence_basis: Dizziness with near-blackout while upright and active is the most common cause of presyncope in the general population and is the easy default explanation for an isolated dizzy spell.

Exertional presyncope plus concurrent exertional chest tightness in an older patient is a red-flag combination for structural heart disease and must be actively screened for from the first turn, even before the exertional pattern is confirmed.
Chest tightness with exertion and near-syncope in an older patient always requires active ACS exclusion regardless of the eventual working diagnosis.
Exertional presyncope can reflect an exertional arrhythmia; absence of palpitations makes this somewhat less likely but it cannot be excluded on history alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: AI Doctor must not settle on simple vasovagal syncope or 'overheating' as the explanation — the combination of exertional presyncope with concurrent chest tightness in an older adult must trigger active red-flag screening for structural heart disease and ACS from the opening message.

differential.evidence_basis: Exertional chest tightness relieved by rest (DI-001) + progressive exertional dyspnoea over 3 months (DI-002) + recurrent exertional light-headedness (DI-003) constitutes the classic SAD (syncope-angina-dyspnoea) symptom triad of severe aortic stenosis.
Clear exertional (not positional/prolonged-standing) trigger with concurrent angina and progressive dyspnoea over months is not consistent with a simple vasovagal or orthostatic mechanism.

Cannot be excluded via telehealth history alone — chest tightness with exertion still requires ACS to remain on the differential and be actively excluded in person, even as aortic stenosis becomes the leading explanation.

No palpitations preceding the episode (DI-004) and a clear, reproducible exertional trigger make a primary arrhythmic cause less likely as the dominant explanation, though it cannot be gold-standard excluded without an ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After eliciting the SAD triad, aortic stenosis (or another critical structural cause of exertional presyncope) should be leading and drive urgent, non-deferrable escalation. Failing to recognise this constellation as high-risk is a diagnostic reasoning failure.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context based on the classic symptom triad; requires urgent in-person examination and echocardiography for confirmation — cannot be confirmed remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final differential in the telehealth context should be presumptive severe aortic stenosis (or unspecified critical structural heart disease) requiring urgent same-day in-person assessment. A response that offers reassurance or defers assessment to a routine appointment indicates failure to integrate the SAD triad appropriately.

red_flags.description: Exertional chest tightness, triggered by walking and relieved by rest, occurring together with presyncope
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exertional angina is one limb of the classic SAD triad of severe aortic stenosis and independently mandates urgent cardiac assessment; combined with presyncope it substantially raises the probability of critical structural heart disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Episode is attributed to heat or simple dizziness; patient continues normal activity; risk of a subsequent syncopal episode with fall injury or sudden cardiac death during exertion.

red_flags.description: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over the preceding three months
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exertional dyspnoea is the third limb of the SAD triad and signals evolving left ventricular decompensation; its presence alongside syncope and angina indicates advanced, high-risk disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Gradual breathlessness dismissed as deconditioning; progression to overt heart failure or sudden cardiac death is not anticipated or safety-netted for.

red_flags.description: Recurrent exertional light-headedness in the weeks preceding today's near-blackout episode
red_flags.clinical_significance: An escalating pattern of exertional presyncope (rather than a single isolated event) indicates worsening fixed-output physiology and increases near-term risk of a completed syncopal event or sudden cardiac death.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Today's event is treated as an isolated one-off; escalating pattern and near-term risk are not recognised; patient continues unsupervised exertion.

red_flags.description: Harsh ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids with slow-rising, small-volume carotid pulse and narrowed pulse pressure on examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: The clinician-confirmatory examination tetrad of aortic stenosis — cannot be obtained via chat and requires in-person auscultation and pulse palpation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth AI Doctor scoring directly, but reinforces why in-person or urgent echocardiography referral is mandatory rather than optional.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor attributes the dizzy spell to heat, dehydration, or simple vasovagal syncope and stops questioning before eliciting the exertional chest tightness and three-month history of progressive dyspnoea.

Patient's framing of the event as a single 'dizzy spell' anchors the AI Doctor on a benign transient cause, obscuring the escalating three-month symptom trajectory that indicates a chronic, worsening structural process.

Isolated presyncope in an older adult is most commonly benign (orthostatic, vasovagal, medication-related) — the AI Doctor may over-weight this common explanation without adjusting for the presence of concurrent exertional angina and dyspnoea, which are much less common and much higher-risk.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation — ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids cannot be heard via chat
Carotid pulse character — slow-rising, small-volume pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus) requires physical palpation
Blood pressure and pulse pressure — cannot be measured remotely without a patient-operated device
Precordial palpation — sustained apical impulse cannot be assessed via chat
12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram — required to confirm the diagnosis and quantify severity
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person medical assessment (ideally ED or urgent care given the SAD triad and sudden cardiac death risk) with cardiovascular examination, ECG, and expedited echocardiography; ambulance transport if further chest pain at rest or any loss of consciousness occurs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that this was a simple dizzy spell and deferring to a routine GP appointment risks a subsequent completed syncopal episode with fall-related injury, or sudden cardiac death, in a patient with an unrecognised critical structural cardiac lesion.
